rationale: The page contains significant factual information regarding public health developments, specifically focusing on Tuberculosis (TB) incidence and types, the HIV prevention drug Lenacapavir, the mechanism of HIV infection, and the elimination of Trachoma in India. These are high-yield topics for competitive examinations.
cleanedText:
5. HEALTH
5.1. DISEASES AND RELATED DEVELOPMENTS
5.1.1. TUBERCULOSIS (TB)
Why in the News?
According to the WHO Global TB Report 2025, India’s TB incidence (new cases emerging each year) reduced by 21%.
About Tuberculosis (TB)
• A contagious airborne disease caused by a bacterium called Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
• Types based on site of Infection:
- Pulmonary TB: Most common form affecting the lungs, and is contagious spreading through airborne droplets through coughing or sneezing.
- Extrapulmonary TB: Occurs outside the lungs, affecting organs like lymph nodes, bones, brain, kidneys, or the pleura, less contagious and spreads within the body from lungs.
- Spinal TB (Pott’s disease): Affects the spine and can cause severe back pain and mobility issues.
• Types based on Response to Drugs:
- Drug-sensitive TB: This form of TB responds well to the standard first-line anti-TB medicines such as isoniazid and rifampicin.
- Drug-Resistant TB: Some forms of TB do not respond to standard medicines.
- Multi Drug Resistant (MDR-TB): Form of TB that does not respond to rifampicin and isoniazid, the two most effective first-line TB drugs.
- Extensively Drug-Resistant (XDR-TB): In this type of TB, the stronger medicines may not work.
5.1.2. HUMAN IMMUNODEFICIENCY VIRUS (HIV)
Why in the News?
US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approves new Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) prevention drug Lenacapavir.
About Lenacapavir
• Lenacapavir is an antiretroviral medicine that is used for HIV prevention as a Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis or PrEP.
- PrEP is a medication that can reduce the risk of HIV infection for individuals who are HIV-negative but are at risk of contracting the virus.
About HIV
• A virus that attacks the body’s immune system.
• Mechanism: HIV damages the immune system by infecting and destroying CD4 cells (CD4 T lymphocytes), a type of white blood cells.
• Transmission: Body fluids of an infected person, including blood, breast milk, semen and vaginal fluids.
- It can also spread from a mother to her baby.
• Treatment: HIV can be prevented and treated with antiretroviral therapy (ART).
- Untreated HIV can progress to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS).
5.1.3. OTHER DISEASES IN NEWS
5.1.3.1. TRACHOMA
India was awarded the Certificate of Elimination of Trachoma as a Public Health Problem by the WHO.
